data.title: 2 REIT-Focused ETFs Delivering Investors Monthly Cash Flow
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

We recently went over two closed-end funds that provide exposure to real estate investment trusts. That included Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) and Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NRO). Today, I wanted to look at two different exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") that also provide REIT exposure. However, these funds aren't leveraged—instead, they come up with other ways to provide significantly higher distribution rates to investors and pay that cash flow out monthly.

Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield ETF (RIET)

  • Distribution Frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution Yield: 10.30% (SEC yield 8.74%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.50%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $88.37 million
  • Structure: Passive ETF

RIET's investment objective:

...is to track the performance, before fees and expenses of the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index, a rules-based index designed to provide diversified exposure to 100 U.S.-list that's estate-related securities that collectively provide income through high dividend yields.

Several key features of RIET include;

  • access to 100 select real estate securities paying high dividend yields
  • "smooth" monthly distributions for shareholders
  • real estate specialist Hoya Capital Real Estate is behind the fund
  • an inflation hedge, which we all know is essential at this time.

This is one I've held for a while now, shortly after the launch of the fund. However, the fund launched at a fairly poor time for REIT exposure when the Fed began raising rates aggressively. That caused significant downward pressure on the fund, and it rather quickly fell around a third in terms of its share price. That said, I personally averaged down aggressively through at the lower prices and have an unrealized gain.

Over this time, I've also been collecting the attractive monthly distribution, which has been quite steady as well. They even raised it in 2023 and haven't cut it despite a few of their underlying positions cutting their own payouts.

With a solid distribution rate of just over 10%, one should clearly be curious where that comes from. The fund itself isn't leveraged to get to those sorts of yields, but instead, they invest in more mREIT exposure—these tend to be higher-yielding investments due to their substantial leverage. REITs, in general, are relatively highly leveraged instruments and then mREITs take it even further.

The current top ten shows us that Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM), Annaly Capital Management (NLY) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD) are the largest mREIT holdings currently. NLY recently raised its dividend, and STWD has been rather resilient for an mREIT. However, the general trend is quite clear that most mREITs tend to cut their high dividends over time rather than raise them.

Here are some other examples of RIET's mREIT holdings that aren't in the top ten but are in the portfolio. The trend here is more dividend cuts over time, as we would expect. Some have even been cut quite recently, though DX is another one of those exceptions where they recently raised their payout.

With that, these come with quite high distribution rates, and it's why RIET can also pay higher rates to its investors.

NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI)

  • Distribution Frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution Yield: 12.19% (SEC yield 3.16%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $28.2 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

IYRI's investment objective is to "generate high monthly income with the potential for equity appreciation." To achieve this, the fund will "distribute high levels of monthly income through a data-driven call option strategy on ETFs that seek to track the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index." The fund is also actively managed, which makes it more flexible than taking a simple indexed approach. The Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index, in this case, is just the reference index, which the underlying portfolio may or may not mirror exactly.

This is a newer fund and is quite small at this time, but it is also the first of its kind that I've seen. Right in the investment policy is how the fund can offer its relatively higher distribution rate, which is through utilizing a call option strategy. I've not seen another fund offer a call writing strategy that is specifically focused on REITs previously. This is primarily where the active part of the portfolio comes from. I don't currently own this fund, but it is on my watchlist of potential ideas.

When it comes to writing options, they are given quite a bit of flexibility as well, noting that they primarily will be writing covered call options on the index or ETFs. They hold individual names, so while it technically wouldn't be covered calls directly because you can't hold an index, and they don't hold the ETF, the underlying portfolio will still primarily reflect the index/ETF. So, indirectly, they are essentially writing covered calls.

They even further allow for the potential to employ an options call spread strategy. In that case, they are buying and selling calls simultaneously. They still look to generate a net-credit from the call spread, but with the long call, it can potentially allow for some further upside participation. Which is to counteract one of the downsides of a call writing strategy where upside participation can be capped if the underlying portfolio is rising too quickly.

As a newer fund, they only have a few distributions under their belt so far.

For its top ten holdings, it is primarily the larger REIT names that we would expect and that a lot of REIT investors are probably more familiar with.

Given these types of holdings, we can see why the 30-day SEC yield comes to around 3%. It is the fund's option strategy that brings it up closer to the 12% payout. The SEC yield would be factoring in the income generated from the dividend yield alone, not the derivatives strategy it employs and the subsequent gains/losses it generates from that.

This is also unlike RIET; with its focus on looking for higher-yielding instruments, we see some more unique names and a higher SEC yield. That isn't a bad thing either, it is just different. In fact, it can make a combination of RIET and IYRI quite appropriate, given their differentiated approaches. With IYRI's call writing strategy, it also changes it from other REIT-focused funds, so it could be appropriate for more high-income-focused investors or in combination with other REIT funds or portfolios.

data.title: Tax Free Municipal Bonds For A Safer Retirement: DMB
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I don't know a single person who truly loves taxes. Some say the exception may be government officials. There are suggestions that they seem to think that taxing something will solve every problem.

Global warming? Tax carbon!

Smoking? Tax it!

Drinking? Tax it!

Taxing a problem is rarely the solution; it generates higher prices or drains valuable dollars from the economy.

However, when the local or regional government needs to get something done, and they don't have the tax dollars to effectively do so, they turn to the debt markets.

Unlike corporate debt, which receives interest that's taxed at your ordinary income tax rate. Municipal debt is exempt from federal taxes, and for many states that have state income tax, if you get municipal debt issued by your state, it will be exempt from state taxes as well. The reason that they need to do this is because there can be large projects like building a bridge, a new school, etc., that need to be funded immediately. The trickle of tax dollars slowly pouring in does not cover the immediate cost. Therefore, a municipal, state, or regional government agency will issue debt to attract investors, allowing them to fund their project and paying over time. The issue you can run into is that some of these projects don't generate revenue of their own, and this could create a large debt burden for local or state entities that can make it more difficult to function day to day, especially if they're already running large deficits. Some cities run big deficits because they assume they're collecting more tax than they're actually collecting. When they raise taxes, they drive investment out of the city. We've seen that large corporations have left Seattle due to higher taxes, leading to even larger deficits than estimated.

One goal of mine when looking at municipal debt is to find municipal debt that's actively revenue-generating on its own. This also means that when I look at a municipal debt fund, I want to see a self-generating focus as well. For example, I'd rather own municipal debt tied to a toll road than I would a municipal debt tied to local education.

Today, I want to look at a fund that focuses on this federal tax-free income and see how it can be a wonderful source of income for your portfolio.

Let's dive in!

Safety From Your Local Government

When the market is panicking, it's a good idea to hold some investments that have low credit risk. BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:DMB), yielding 3.7%, is a closed-end fund that invests in municipal bonds. Historically, municipal bonds have a significantly lower default rate than equally rated corporate bonds. We highlighted its benefits previously, and it has outperformed the market since.

We expect DMB to keep chugging along despite market volatility. The reason is that municipal bonds have very long terms. The average maturity for DMB's portfolio is slightly under 20 years. Source

The credit quality is high, with more than 50% of the portfolio having credit ratings of A or higher. Approximately 75% of the portfolio is "investment grade":

This makes DMB a great candidate to hold in a world where investors are fearful, and interest rates are declining.

The best part is that DMB is trading at about a 10% discount to NAV, allowing us to invest in this portfolio at a lower price than if we bought it directly.

The last time I discussed DMB, the most common objection was that the yield was lower than what we typically target – which is true; DMBs' distribution is relatively low. As NAV recovers, we expect that DMB will join peers who have already raised their dividends. As we're entering into a period where there could be turmoil in the markets and the risk of a recession is high, having a fund that's likely to start raising its distribution and have upside potential when everything else is falling is worth its weight in gold.

DMB has room to more than double its distribution just to match peers in the sector. DMB's restraint with the distribution is likely one reason why it's trading at a larger discount to NAV. It also means that as interest rates come down, municipal bond prices recover. DMB has retained more capital and will see a greater upside than its peers. Ultimately, this will allow it to pay out a higher distribution when management decides it's time to raise.

Investors who are patient and are willing to delay gratification will be rewarded. For less patient investors, Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD) is an alternative option that's paying a higher distribution right now and has a very similar portfolio. Nuveen hiked the distribution of many of their muni-bond funds as a measure to reduce their discount to NAV. Is it sustainable? Only time will tell, but it does give the less patient investor a higher-yielding option.

Maybe you want to have a helping of both!

Either way, it's time to have municipals as part of your portfolio.

Conclusion

DMB is not an investment designed to outperform the market radically over the long term. An investment, it's designed to provide you with stable and steady federally tax-free income over the long run. Investing in the government's debt can be highly rewarding and a stable option. There's a reason why Treasury notes are viewed as exceptionally low risk – it's very rare that the government ever defaults on its debt. You can invest in individual municipal bonds on your own or use a fund like DMB and NAD to invest in a wide array of them, gaining exposure to many different great income-producing opportunities.

In the end, I want your retirement to be flooded with income, saturating every aspect of your financial situation and providing you with a reservoir of excess income that you can then use to reinvest in your portfolio, save for hard days ahead, or generously give to others and to the causes you support. The greatest aspect of being an income investor is that the cash that arrives in your account is 100% yours to do whatever you want with. It's not in the hands of a management team who thinks they know better. It's not in the hands of a portfolio manager. It's in your hands. The best place for it to be.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Tariffied? 3 Of The Smartest Dividend Stocks To Buy Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Last week was quite something, wasn't it?

As I have written in prior articles, it was the most stressful week in my career, as we were dealing with a myriad of political and macroeconomic news that created market-moving headlines on a daily basis (literally).

Using the handy overview below, we see, once again, what we went through, as not a single day was anywhere close to 'normal.' On Monday, the Cboe Volatility Index made it to 60, on Tuesday, the S&P 500 fell 1.6% on massive volumes, followed by a 9.5% rally on Wednesday.

Then, on Thursday, the market fell by 3.5%, followed by a jump of almost 2% on Friday. Also, during this week, the dollar lost nearly 3%.

To make matters worse, the numbers above do not show the intraday swings that traders and investors had to deal with. Looking at closing prices is one thing. The drama that comes between 9:30 AM and 4:00 PM is another thing.

When including low-to-high swings, the market has the highest volatility since the Great Financial Crisis, as even the 2020 pandemic had more predictable headlines. I don't want to sound repetitive, but that's quite something.

Even after President Trump put a pause on new tariffs except for China, the market remained nervous, as the biggest risk became confidence in the United States and the elevated risks of a recession tied to the trade war with China.

I believe the quote below hits the nail on the head in light of rising longer-term bond yields despite a weaker dollar and lower inflation:

“Confidence outside the US on the quality and leadership of US assets across equity, fixed income and currency has been damaged,” said Nathan Thooft at Manulife Investment Management in Boston, which oversees $160 billion. “The question remains is this a temporary hit or a secular shift? We still believe the former. But that doesn’t dismiss the fact there are large asset owners who are looking for substitutes and diversification for safe haven assets.” - Bloomberg

In light of this, it's quite interesting to note that while the S&P 500 is down 9% year-to-date, the EURO STOXX 50 ETF (FEZ) is up 10%. Roughly half of these gains are coming from a strong euro. But still...

Now, the question on everyone's mind is the same.

Is This A Buyable Bottom?

I don't know. And neither does anyone else.

Even Wall Street's smartest people cannot tell you if this is a bottom.

Anyone who pretends otherwise is lying.

But that's OK.

What we can do is assess the risk/reward, which is something we always do, as it's our best weapon against uncertainty.

Goldman Sachs, for example, uses learnings from prior bear markets to assess what it takes for stocks to bottom.

For starters, there is a huge difference between cyclical and event-driven bear markets. The first one is very serious, while the other is often nasty and short. Essentially, it explains why everyone is so nervous, as avoiding a steep recession is critical to keep this an event-driven bear market.

The average cyclical and event-driven bear markets generally tend to fall around 30%, although they differ in terms of duration. Cyclical bear markets last an average of around two years and take about five years to fully rebound to their starting point, while the event-driven ones tend to last around eight months and recover in about a year. - Goldman Sachs

The good news is that most bear markets recover within a year. However, they often require triggers. Goldman noted four of them:

  • Stocks need to be attractively valued.
  • Investor sentiment/positioning needs to be very pessimistic.
  • The stock market/economy needs policy support.
  • We need second-derivative tailwinds. This means the rate of change (growth rates) needs to improve. For example, economic contraction becomes less severe.

Let's look into this.

For now, I believe we're in an event-driven scenario, as President Trump's tariffs triggered the global sell-off. Right off the bat, that's good to know.

With regard to valuations, we're basically still in a tricky scenario, as we need to be selective.

The S&P 500 trades at 19x earnings, which is not very cheap compared to historical averages. However, the equal-weight S&P 500 is far from overvalued at 15x earnings, as the market-weighted S&P 500 is still very top-heavy.

Meanwhile, mid-cap stocks are very cheap.

In other words, we are not necessarily at levels that would suggest the end of a correction, especially because tariffs could pressure earnings, which is often an overlooked factor.

However, many value stocks are certainly cheap.

So, what about investor sentiment?

Investors are bearish. As of April 9, 59% of retail investors expected lower stock prices over the next six months, almost twice as many as the historic average of the AAII survey. That's the kind of sentiment you want to see when buying stocks.

We are seeing similar developments in the Fear & Greed index, which is at 13, a number indicative of 'Extreme Fear.' While it's off the lows, it is still below its 2024 lows and driven by poor market breadth, a lot of put buying, and elevated volatility.

So, what about policy support?

This is where it gets tricky.

  • Although inflation has come down, we are unlikely to get monetary support, as the Fed will have to wait and see what the impact of tariffs will be on inflation (and growth). Right now, the market expects one rate cut in June, another one in July, and another one in September. However, conviction isn't high, as we can see below. Personally, I do not believe we will get a cut unless fiscal policy risks have faded.

  • The biggest risk is policy headwinds, as new tariffs could increase uncertainty, boost recession odds, and cause other issues. However, I believe policy risks could be bullish. For starters, China tariffs have reached such a high level that we are basically looking at a trade embargo between China and the U.S. Even if tariffs were to go to 1,000% (just as an example), not a lot would change. Moreover, because China is much more dependent on the U.S. than the other way around, I believe China will be forced into talks that could cause some very positive headlines. In general, I believe the market's reaction to bond turmoil will make it more likely that tariff risks fall, as the U.S. is not in a good spot to deal with elevated long-term bonds.

Last but not least, growth rates matter.

However, right now, that's not the most important indicator, as weird as that may sound. After all, we're still in an event-driven correction. It all depends on policies and their impact on growth.

Nobody really knows what inflation will look like next month or how badly earnings will be hit by tariffs.

For now, its real GDP is expected to contract. However, since early April, growth expectations have rebounded a bit. If this continues, this factor could be bullish as well. But then again, it's a bit early to make that call.

So, where do we stand?

For now, we're dealing with an event-driven correction, triggered by tariffs and amplified by uncertainty. While some value stocks are attractively priced, especially in the mid-cap space, the broader market isn't super cheap.

Sentiment, on the other hand, is deeply bearish, which is typically a positive contrarian signal.

Meanwhile, policy support remains uncertain, as the Fed is boxed in by inflation risks and political noise.

Still, I believe policy risks could ultimately work in the market's favor.

Growth data is mixed, and while it's not the main story right now, any rebound there could add fuel to a recovery.

With that said, going into this week, I want to share three dividend ideas with you that, I believe, are great buys during this uncertainty, as they all bring something to the table that could make them even stronger players in the years ahead than before the tariff turmoil started(!).

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) - One Of My Favorite Dividend Growers With Re-Shoring Benefits

Last week, I expanded my investment in Old Dominion Freight Line by 65%, making it my fourth-largest position, accounting for 5.2% of my portfolio.

This was based on a number of reasons, including its poor performance, as it's a cyclical less-than-truckload ("LTL") carrier. Whenever recession fears start to rise, investors dump these stocks.

With that said, I dislike the trucking industry, in general, as it's capital-intensive, very competitive, and prone to disruptions like labor shortages.

The good news is that LTL is different, as companies make shorter trips and can use their distribution network to build a competitive edge. That's why ODFL has a "narrow moat" tag from Morningstar, which is very unique in its industry.

This narrow moat is based on 261 service/distribution centers in 48 states. The majority of these centers are owned by the company, which gives it a major real estate footprint. This network makes it the second-largest LTL provider behind FedEx Freight, which is set to become an independent company through a spin-off.

ODFL is so efficient that it has grown its revenue by 10.3% per year since 2004. This is entirely organic, meaning no major M&A has boosted these results.

Moreover, it is, by far, the best LTL provider, as it has a claims ratio of a mere 0.1% and an on-time performance of 99%. It has also won the Mastio Quality Award 15 years in a row.

This does not just sound good, but also indicates pricing power.

Using the Mastio numbers below, we see that ODFL has a superior service level, which comes with pricing power, as most peers have worse service levels and only barely lower prices.

This comes with inflation protection, as ODFL used to charge close to $300 per shipment in 2015. Now, that number is close to $500.

Moreover, it has an operating ratio of roughly 73%, which is super efficient, as most trucking companies have operating ratios close to 100%. The operating ratio indicates how much it costs to run the business, as a percentage of total revenues.

Railroads usually have operating ratios in the 60% range. A 75% number is truly phenomenal.

Even better, the company has negative net debt, meaning it has more cash than gross debt. This, too, adds a layer of stability and allows the company to invest in growth without having to tap the debt market.

The only bad news is that ODFL yields just 0.7%.

Fortunately, this dividend has a payout ratio of a mere 19% and a five-year CAGR of 34%.

Moreover, despite losing more than a third of its value, the company is still outperforming the market, as its ten-year total return is a staggering 555%, a mile above the market's impressive 202% return.

Going forward, I believe ODFL will continue to beat the market, as it could be one of the biggest winners of economic re-shoring. Even if President Trump were to eliminate most tariffs, re-shoring will likely accelerate, as companies de-risk their supply chains somewhat.

As LTL companies have an industrial focus, they will benefit tremendously. ODFL, especially, will be in a good spot, as it can use its balance sheet to expand more efficiently, while its service quality will help it to win new business deals.

With regard to its valuation, the company now trades at its 10-year average P/E ratio of 27.9x. While it needs to be seen how bad the impact of tariffs will be, analysts expect 18% and 17% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027.

If we get a more sustainable uptrend, I expect ODFL to be a major winner and enjoy a lot more business wins in the years ahead.

While making it my #4 position made my portfolio more volatile, it's a 'bet' I feel extremely good about.

ONEOK (OKE) - 'Mission-Critical' Income

I thought about this pick quite a bit, as I have discussed ONEOK a lot this year.

However, I think it deserves even more attention, as I think investors who sold the stock made a big mistake.

While one may make the case that ONEOK was a bit overvalued when it ran to almost $120 per share in November, the 30% sell-off since then seems to be overblown.

This brings me to the reason why OKE is included in this article.

Because of the trade war with China, the market feared it could lead to less Chinese demand for natural gas and natural gas liquids, which are two commodities (commodity groups, to be precise) that are needed by the Chinese.

For example, propane, which is a natural gas liquid, has seen massive export growth, reaching 2 million barrels per day at the end of 2024.

Generally speaking, the country requires a lot of natural gas liquids, which also include butane, isobutane, pentane, and ethane. These are used in a wide range of operations, including industrial production, residential heating, and transportation.

In light of the trade war, demand is expected to fall due to rising recession risks and trade embargoes.

However, China does not have alternatives, as Reuters reported:

Russia's exporters of liquefied petroleum gas are unlikely to be able to provide enough LPG to make up for U.S. supplies disrupted by tariffs, traders said on Thursday, citing a lack of infrastructure.

Industry insiders said that Chinese petrochemical makers that buy U.S. LPG worth $11 billion annually are poised to cut output or shut for maintenance in the coming weeks as retaliatory tariffs on U.S. imports drive up costs. - Reuters

While recessions will temporarily hurt demand, I see no damage to America's success in natural gas liquids. In fact, I am super bullish on it, as there's no country on earth in a better spot to service the world's rising demand for these commodities than the U.S. For example, the Permian Basin alone produces more than half of OPEC's entire natural gas liquids volume.

That's where ONEOK comes in, as it owns a 60,000-mile pipeline network that transports natural gas liquids, refined products, crude oil, and natural gas.

Close to 40% of its revenue this year is expected to come from natural gas liquids, followed by gathering/processing activities (28%), and refined/crude oil shipments (27%).

The best thing about this network is that it makes ONEOK 'mission-critical' in servicing domestic and export demand. It also benefits from mostly take-or-pay contracts, which protect the company even if volumes were to decline.

That is very important, as current oil and gas prices are very subdued.

Even during the 2014/2025 oil price crash and the 2020/2021 pandemic, the company did not cut its dividend and grew its EBITDA. This year, EBITDA is expected to grow by 21%, potentially followed by 10% growth in 2026.

It is also deploying at least $2.3 billion in growth CapEx this year to exploit strong secular growth:

Key projects have included NGL pipeline expansions in the Bakken and Permian Basins, additional NGL fractionation capacity in Mont Belvieu and the Mid-Continent, refined products pipeline expansions into the Denver market and natural gas storage expansions in Oklahoma and Texas. Most recently, we announced an LPG export project joint venture with MPLX. With LPG exports added to our service mix, we will provide an integrated NGL wellhead-to-water solution for our customers enhancing our product offerings. - OKE 4Q24 Earnings Call

With regard to its dividend, currently yielding 5.0%, the dividend is expected to grow by 3-4% per year going forward. I do not expect any major recession to derail these plans.

The next one is a bit more cyclical.

United Rentals (URI) - Equipment To Make America Great Again

Growth expectations have taken a hit in the U.S., as we discussed in the first part of this article. This has hit cyclical stocks like ODFL.

Another stock that has been hit is United Rentals, a fast-growing company focused on equipment rentals. Founded in 1997, it has used high organic growth and aggressive M&A to capture a market share of 15%, with a large footprint in specialty equipment.

Because the company has such a large footprint and equipment for a wide range of tasks, including infrastructure, residential, pipelines, chemicals, industrial manufacturing, power generation, and more, it benefits from higher growth expectations.

As a result of economic expectations coming down recently, URI is trading 35% below its 52-week high, one of its biggest drawdowns in years. Despite this, it has returned more than 520% over the past ten years.

Moreover, the company plans another >50 new locations in 2025, which underscores its methodical expansion into underserved markets.

These greenfield sites often focus on specialty products like matting (acquired via the 2022 BlueLine deal) or climate solutions, which come with high margins and benefit from secular trends like infrastructure spending and workplace safety regulations.

With regard to shareholder distributions, the company enjoys a super healthy balance sheet with a 1.8x leverage ratio, $2.8 billion in liquidity, and no major maturities until 2027.

Because United Rentals has matured in recent years, it started a dividend program in 1Q23. It recently hiked this dividend by 10%. It currently yields 1.2% with a payout ratio of a mere 15%.

In other words, there's a lot of dividend growth potential, which comes with aggressive buybacks, as URI has bought back 9% of its shares over the past three years alone.

Although the current environment is tough, I believe URI is a critical part of Trump's plan to revive American manufacturing and broaden economic growth.

Especially in light of data center growth, pipeline shortages, factory construction, and other large projects, URI has a front seat in America's economy.

Trading at 13.4x earnings, the company is expected to grow its EPS by 11-12% in 2026 and 2027.

While these numbers are subject to change (now more than ever), I am very bullish on URI and would be a buyer if I did not already own Caterpillar (CAT), which is a correlated play.

Takeaway

Last week was one for the history books, as we saw brutal volatility, policy chaos, and a lot of fear.

I can't say with certainty whether we've seen the bottom, but I can say this: the setup increasingly looks like an event-driven correction, not a prolonged downturn.

Valuations in some corners of the market, especially mid-caps and value stocks, are attractive, sentiment is bearish (which is good), and policy risks might actually become tailwinds.

That's why I'm using this environment to buy quality stocks that bring something special to the table.

In this article, we discussed three of them. One with a high yield and two with low yields but aggressive growth.

Going forward, I expect all of these stocks to beat the market and deliver consistent dividend growth.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is prolonged policy uncertainty.

If tariff tensions escalate further or if the Federal Reserve has to delay rate cuts due to sticky inflation, market volatility could remain elevated and pressure the stock market.

data.title: Seritage Growth Properties: Special Situation Buy
data.description:
Summary

Seritage Growth Properties (NYSE:SRG) was spun off from Sears Holdings back in 2015. Currently, it is in the liquidation stage. Shareholders have approved a Plan of Sale to dispose all of its assets back in October 2022. This has transformed SRG into a liquidating trust.

Investment Thesis

I believe that the market is overbearish on SRG, creating a potential mispricing. From my calculation, the asset value actually exceeds its current market capitalization using a NAV calculation and deducting its credit obligations. Essentially, the market is valuing SRG at a discount relative to its estimated net asset value. Of course, this is dependent on the management's execution of the Plan of Sale. If they are able to sell the remaining properties quickly, the IRR would be higher to shareholders.

However, this investment is without risk as it has a negative carry. It is a company that is slowly bleeding. SRG is cash burning as well. The company reported negative profit of $154 million in 2024. This is mainly driven by lower rental revenue compared to its high cost. Moreover, its maturity of debt is approaching next year, which also caused the heavy discount.

What is driving the mispricing?

Currently, SRG has a market capitalization of ~$150M, and is trading at 0.37x P/B. There are a few factors driving the mispricing:

Poor Outlook

SRG's poor financials are hard to ignore. Its earnings and cash are diminishing. Moreover, funds are also forced to sell the small-cap due to either mandates and its unattractive quality of a REIT with no distributions and a finite lifespan. Management also warned market headwinds, which led to investors taking a walk away from this investment. However, I believe, this selling pressure causes the share price to fall more than it should.

Hard Deadlines

July 2025 is the debt maturity, and this created fear to investors. However, management has negotiated an extension of its term loan to July 2026. However, this scenario would be insufficient for risk-averse investors, which led to this strong pessimism.

Insider Alignment

Eddie Lampert, the former Sears CEO, still owns a good ~24% of SRG shares. We can still take some comfort that they would act in their own favor to sell these assets and maximize the remaining value. Therefore, the market seems to be fearful and for good reasons. However, a combination of the aforementioned factors and a deeper dive into the properties would paint a story that is worth looking into more.

NAV Analysis

As of 2024, SRG's portfolio has a cumulative 17 properties with ownership of ~1.3 million sq ft, sitting on 220 acres of land. The properties are split into 10 wholly owned (~0.9 million sq ft., 166 acres) and 7 joint ventures (~0.8 million sq. ft., 108 acres).

SRG's real estate portfolio comprises both wholly owned properties and joint venture (JV) assets. Wholly owned properties are fully controlled and operated, meaning 100% of their rental income, expenses, and sale proceeds accrue directly to the company. In contrast, joint venture properties are co-owned with third-party partners such as Simon Property Group or Howard Hughes Corporation.

In order to derive an implied valuation of the properties, I did a bottom-up perspective with various assumptions. A few variables that have to be figured out are: (1) Rent/Square Feet, (2) Fair Expense Ratio, and (3) Cap Rate. Once we have these 3 figures across each property, we can then find out the fair value of each property in a rough sense. Do note that the properties are valued in a context where, should the properties are running in a normalized condition (not in a liquidation), how much would the properties be worth.

Rent per Square Foot (rent/sf) for each SRG property was assigned based on a combination of market comparables and property quality and parts of company disclosure. As the company did not do a full disclosure for each property, the best guess is to use logic and market comps to derive them. Looking at SRG's recent lease rates for one of its properties reaching $56/sf and its disclosure range of rent/sf to be between $20 and $58. Therefore, my assumptions would be within this range. Fully leased assets and great locations such as Braintree, San Diego, and Redmond are assigned as $40/sf. However, properties like Aventura and King of Prussia, are mid-tier with partial leasing and strong suburban fundamentals, I assigned $30-37/sf. Weaker properties, such as Clearwater and Altamonte Springs, were assigned $25-30/sf. Thus, this is accounting for tenant quality and occupancy status.

As stated above, the expense ratio is set at 30% to reflect normalized operating margin. In this context, a 30% fair expense ratio is used, which implies a 70% NOI margin. Looking at peers like Regency Center Corporation and Federal Realty Investment Trust, their estimated NOI margins are around ~70%. This means that property-level operating expenses typically fall around 30% of gross rent.

Lastly, the cap rates are assigned similarly with similar methodology as rent/sf based on its riskiness, and quality of properties. This means that a property that is fully leased deserves a lower cap rate, as it would imply a higher valuation. The valuation range is 6.5%-8.5%. Cap rates of 6.5-7.0% are given to stabilized, income-producing properties. 7.5-8.5% are for partial leasing. These figures are anchored based on a disclosure by SRG in Q1 2025, where 7.7% cap rate was disclosed in SRG's own transaction data.

Calculation of NAV

Based on the calculation above, we are getting $282 million for just properties alone. This is lower than its book value of $358 million, which makes sense as it accounts for non-revenue generating assets and conservative purposes. Then we can add the cash of $97.5 million and land of $65 million, and we treat the rest as unclaimable for conservative stake. This gives us a total of ~$445 million ($65 million + $282 million + $97.5 million) as implied valuation. Then we minus total liabilities of $271 million, giving us a net asset value of $174 million. Currently, SRG's market cap is $150 million. This means that there is a potential ~16% return of value that the market is mispricing. This gives us an NAV of $3/share ($174 million / 56.255 million in shares outstanding).

Asymmetric Risk/Reward

SRG's deep decline has already priced in substantial bad news, and any positive could swing the share price upwards. Assuming a timeline of roughly 1 year for the liquidation to play out (mid to end-2026) as that is where its term loan maturity hits.

In the Bull Case, NAV $7/share. This is achieved assuming the financials in SRG's are fairly valued, and this would give us a total asset of $674 million with total liabilities of $272 million. The net asset would be $402 million ($674 million - $272 million), deriving ~$7/share. That is a ~160% gain. But the assumptions are unlikely to hit unless the stars are fully aligned where maturity is extended again and interest rates fall quicker than expected, giving these properties room to transform from a liquidating trust into an operating business. However, I know that the probability of hitting this is pretty unlikely to hit.

As for the base case, NAV is $3/share. This means that investing would see a 17% gain in one year, which is not bad. The base case essentially suggests you won't lose money and could make a modest return. If SRG achieves a fair liquidation, and assuming all the assumptions above play out in the calculation of NAV, hitting $3/share can be expected.

Lastly, a bear case would be actually $0/share. If the worst happens when the properties are not able to be sold out until the maturity happens, common shareholders get nothing at all. So, the downside is essentially losing your entire investment. But of this, this is dependent that the management is unable to liquidate these properties, which, I believe, is also quite unlikely.

Conclusion

As, this particular investment idea is definitely more speculative than my other articles because the nature of this investment is both on balance sheet investing basis and dependent on management's ability to sell these properties before the maturity hits. But do also note that my assumptions of getting these properties fair value are pretty conservative, as the rent/sf is not aggressive at all.

You can also think of it as the market is pricing this investment like an out-of-the-money call option, but in actuality it is an in-the-money as the assets are worth more than the market capitalization. Lastly, insider ownership is still quite high at 24%, and I don't think the management have any plans to give up on them.

data.title: Greenback Remains On The Defensive, While Stocks And Bonds Come Back Better Bid
data.description:
Overview

It seems the dramatic reaction in the capital markets and the collapse of consumer confidence (and erosion of public support for its policies) prompted the Trump administration to postpone the reciprocal tariffs. And after the markets closed Friday, it announced on its website that smartphones, computers and other popular consumer electronics, and machines used to fabricate semiconductors were exempt from the so-called reciprocal tariffs and should have been part of the April 2 announcement. The administration was quick to say that coming tariffs on semiconductors, which it knows are coming even though the investigation is not completed, will cover the consumer electronic products. An announcement on semiconductor tariffs is expected soon. The repeated adjustments seem to reduce the credibility of claims of some "master plan" unfolding.

Comparisons of the US with emerging markets in recent days seems nearly as extreme as the epithets against the UK during the short-lived Truss affair in September 2022. While it helped us call sterling's low, the dollar's downtrend is intact, but the firmer tone in US index futures and bonds has removed some of the last week's manic feel. Asia Pacific markets rallied today after a three-week, 8.5% slide. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up 2% today after falling 11.5% in its three-week drop. Benchmark 10-year yields in Europe mostly 3-7 bps lower and peripheral premiums are narrowing. UK Gilts are outperforming with a nearly nine basis point decline. The 10-year US Treasury (US10Y) reached almost 4.60% before the weekend and is slipping below 4.44%. Gold is consolidating near the record high set before the weekend, slightly below $3246. May WTI is firm near $62 as prices continue to consolidate with last Thursday's range (~$58.75-$63.35).

USD: The Dollar Index recovered from the spike down to almost 99.00 in early European trading before the weekend, and in the volatile session, met sellers on the move to almost 100.40. The session high was near 100.75 in Asia Pacific turnover. US equities recovered and yields pulled back but only after the 10-year made new highs for the move near 4.59%. It has remained below Friday's settlement (~110.10) today but also above 99.20. With US bonds and stocks trading higher, the tone is less manic. After last week's inflation gauges, attention turns back to the real sector. Retail sales, industrial production, and housing starts are featured. The high frequency data, however, seems less significant now for at least two reasons. First, some will be flattered, like retail sales (helped by stronger than expected auto sales) and some data will not show the impact of the headwind coming to the US. Bloomberg estimated that despite the postponement of the reciprocal tariffs, because of the hike on China, the average effective tariff is down from 27% but at 24% it still poses a threat to growth and prices.

Euro: Before the weekend, the euro settled a little above the middle of the day's range and still finished almost 1.4% higher and rallying 2.3% the day before. The euro reached almost $1.1475, its highest level in three years, in late Asia/early European activity following China's announcement of another (and it says last) increase in its tariff on the US. The euro was capped near $1.1410 in North America and spent most of the afternoon straddling the $1.13 area. The weekly gain of more than 3.5% was also the largest weekly advance since November 2022. Today, it saw the session low early near $1.11280 and reached $1.1425 as European markets were opening. It looks poised to challenge the pre-weekend high. The eurozone sees the aggregate February industrial production figures and Germany's ZEW survey tomorrow, but the highlight of the week ECB meeting on Thursday. There was some debate about an April cut, but the market has clearly become more confident of a quarter-point rate cut. The growth impact from the tariffs, and the price implications of the euro's strength and the drop in oil prices, encourages such views. In early March, the swaps market was discounting a little more than 40 bp of cuts in the remainder of the year. Now, a little more than 75 bps is priced into the swaps curve.

CNY: Rather than a sharp devaluation of the yuan by Beijing in response to the tariffs, it is the dollar that fell. In the past three sessions, the dollar fell by about 2% against the offshore yuan. Ahead of the weekend, the dollar fell to a six-day low against the offshore yuan. And in the face of the escalation of the tariff war, the offshore yuan rose for the first time in four weeks. Its greenback is snapping the three-day decline and is up almost 0.3% to CNH7.31 in the European morning have been up to almost CNH7.3175. The PBOC has not been taken off its course (relatively stable yuan against the dollar). Since Trump's inauguration, the onshore and offshore yuan have depreciated by about 0.40% and 0.60%, respectively. We suspect when push comes to shove, Beijing will choose more domestic stimulus over a large devaluation against the dollar to blunt the impact of the tariffs. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at CNY7.2100 (CNY7.2087 before the weekend, which was the first lower dollar fix in seven sessions). March trade surplus was larger than expected at $102.6 bln, up from $58.6 bln in March 2024. China's trade dominance has increased since Trump's initial tariffs in 2018, but today's figures were likely bolstered by attempts from exporters and importers to get ahead of the tariffs. Exports are up about 12.4% year-over-year, while imports are 4.3% lower. Aggregate financing jumped more than expected in March, reaching CNH15.18 trillion, year-to-date, compared with CNY12.8 trillion as of March 2024. However, while bank lending increased, the lion's share of the improvement came from the so-called shadow banking (e.g., non-bank financial institutions). Bank lending increased by around CNY222 bln of the increase from March 2024, while the shadow banking accounted for the remainder.

JPY: In the past three sessions, the dollar fell nearly 4.2% against the Japanese yen to approach JPY142.00. After retesting the low in early North American trade, short covering helped lift the greenback to almost JPY144.20, where sellers pushed it back toward JPY143.60. The dollar is trading within the pre-weekend range today. A break of JPY142 may require a further pullback in US yields, even though the volatility of US rates and the dollar appears to have loosened the relationship between the US 10-year yield and the exchange rate. Our working hypothesis is that it may take a bigger increase in the US premium to compensate investors. There is much talk that Japanese investors have been selling Treasuries. It is possible, but where can it be documented? Capital flows are reported with a lag. It is a bit like US jobs. The media reports large layoffs, not the hiring. Market scuttlebutt is about Japanese selling, but they do that every day, but there are also Japanese buyers. One of the most authoritative sources of Japanese purchases of foreign bonds reported last week that they were net buyers in February of about $9.6 bln of US bonds, which hardly sheds light on recent activity.

GBP: Sterling is extended last week's gain to approach $1.3200 after it reached $1.3145 last Friday. It had spent most of the pre-weekend session and spent most of the European session and the entire North American session consolidating in choppy activity. It was unable to rise much above $1.3130 in North America and recorded a low about a cent lower shortly after Europe closed the books them for the week. Still, a higher close today allows sterling to extend its advance for the fifth consecutive session, the longest run since last November. The UK employment report is due tomorrow, and the March CPI is on Wednesday. Barring a significant surprise, the market's confidence of a rate cut at the Bank of England's May 8 meeting. It is fully discounted. More telling of the shift in market sentiment is the extent of rate cuts now anticipated for this year. As recently as March 25, the swaps market has about 40 bp of cuts discounted, which is one quarter-point cut and a 60% of second. Now, there are nearly 90 bp of cuts priced into the swaps curve. That puts the year-end rate near 3.55% from over 4% in late March.

CAD: The US dollar last traded above CAD1.44 on April 1 and ahead of the weekend fell to CAD1.3840, its lowest level since last November. It has made a marginal new low today near CAD1.3830. For the third consecutive session, the greenback settled below its lower Bollinger Band, which is found around CAD1.3910 today. Chart support may be around CAD1.3800. Canada reports its March CPI tomorrow. The Bank of Canada meets Wednesday. Through last month, the central bank slashed its policy rate starting June 2024 from 5.0% to 2.75%. The market flirted with the idea of another cut this week. The odds a week ago were near 66% and have been halved, even though one might think the strength of the Canadian dollar (the best level since last October) may encourage a pre-cautionary cut given the likely tariff headwind. Still, proximity of the April 28 election may be a consideration.

AUD: The Australian dollar posted a key upside reversal in the middle of last week by making a new (five-year) low and then recovered to settle above the previous day's high. Follow-through buying lifted the Aussie to $0.6300 ahead of the weekend; a 6.5% rally from Wednesday's low (~$0.5915). The rally has been extended to almost $0.6345 today. The risk-reward changes as the top of the four-month range is approached around $0.6400. The Reserve Bank of Australia's minutes from the meeting earlier this month may draw some passing interesting tomorrow, but the importance will be in the clues to the bar for next month's meeting. The futures market is getting aggressive. It is discounting nearly a 40% chance of a 50 bp cut. It is now pricing almost 120 bp of cuts this year. That is nearly twice as much as was being discounted as recently as March 27.

MXN: The greenback snapped a three-week advance against the peso last week and fell about 0.60%. The dollar posted a bearish key reversal in the middle of last week, but there was no follow-through selling. It ended up consolidating for the past two sessions. It is trading with a heavier bias today. A break of MXN20.17 is needed to open up the downside and spur a test to MXN20.00. The month's low was set April 3 near MXN19.84, which at the time was a little above the 200-day moving average, which comes in now slightly below MXN19.88.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Normal Deleveraging And Trade Wars
data.description:
Deleveraging gained powerful momentum early in the week, with global debt markets at the cusp of "seizing up." 90-day Tariff Pause.

April 9 - Bloomberg (Hadriana Lowenkron and Daniel Flatley): "Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent played down a selloff in US Treasuries, saying that there was nothing systemic at play, and also served warning against China not to attempt to devalue its exchange rate in retaliation for American tariff hikes. 'There's one of these deleveraging convulsions that's going on right now in the markets,' Bessent said on Fox Business, adding that he'd witnessed those very often in his decades-long hedge-fund career. 'It's in the fixed-income market. There are some very large leverage players who are experiencing losses - that are having to deleverage'… 'I believe that there is nothing systemic about this - I think that it is an uncomfortable but normal deleveraging that's going on in the bond market,' Bessent said."

"Bond Market Turbulence Lifts 30-Year Yield Most Since March 2020." "US government debt sells off as hedge funds cut down on risk." "Is China dumping U.S. Treasuries?" "Bond Analysts Debate If China Had Role in Treasuries Swings." "Bond Markets Retreat as US Treasuries Lead Yield Jump Worldwide." "No Emerging Market Is Spared From Trade War Losses on Dollar Debt." "Asian Stocks Tumble Most Since 2008 on Global Recession Worries."

There was nothing "normal" about this week's deleveraging - nothing if not systemic. At week's end, an optimist might think this was just another bout of instability soon to be forgotten. There was the October 2022 Liz Truss deleveraging fiasco. March 2023 saw the bank run eruption and mini crisis. The more germane deleveraging erupted with the unwind of yen "carry trade" leverage back on August 5th, 2024.

August deleveraging was thwarted by Bank of Japan comments on August 6th, soon followed by a dovish pivot by Chair Powell at Jackson Hole. As markets recovered, the narrative immediately shifted to, with yen "carry trade" leverage unwound with minimal impact, the coast was clear to get back to leveraging and speculating.

I was skeptical of the whole unwind narrative. There was likely a significant unwind of currency and swaps positioning. But there was no way the massive leverage in higher yielding debt instruments (funded with cheap yen borrowings) could have been unwound over a few days or even weeks. Such deleveraging would have been systemic and deeply impactful to global market liquidity. I actually believe that unwind is ongoing. It's worth noting that local currency Brazilian 10-year yields increased 350 bps between August and year-end, to 15%. Yields were up 100 bps in Mexico, 200 bps in Colombia, and 300 bps in Turkey.

A Friday WSJ (Jon Sindreu) article captured today's deleveraging fears: "The basis trade, said UBS strategist Michael Cloherty, has 'become the scary monster under the bed that gets blamed for everything.'" There are all varieties of leveraged speculation in myriad instruments across fixed income in the U.S. and globally. Leveraged speculation has proliferated for decades, and it's reasonable to think in terms of tens of Trillions of leverage globally.

There was stress almost across the board this week, including "carry trades" and "basis trades" and especially the "swaps" derivatives marketplace. Barring global markets seizing up, deleveraging will be an ongoing challenge over the coming weeks and months. This week was merely a notably rocky start to the process, and I doubt much progress has been made in unwinding the massive "basis trade" in Treasuries. There are a few dominant "basis trade" operators that won't be able to come out of their positions without Federal Reserve assistance.

From Bloomberg Intelligence's Brian Meehan: "There's a Basis-Trade Blowup, Just Not the One People Expect." "Fears of a massive unwinding of long positions in the cash Treasury/Treasury futures basis trade appear overblown, as the relative performance of cash vs. futures doesn't indicate large liquidations. Also, open interest in Treasury futures hasn't signaled steep declines witnessed in previous blowups."

Ten-year Treasury yields spiked 50 bps this week, the largest weekly jump since the week of August 16, 2001 - a bond shellacking Bloomberg at the time called "the biggest weekly loss in at least 24 years." That was also the week of an Alan Greenspan quote of historic note: "If I answered that in a way which you think you understand what I said, I made a mistake. Remember, the purpose of monetary policy is to address the structure of financial markets - that's what we do." I appreciate that Chair Powell never succumbs to such blather. But everyone sure loved it when the "Maestro" was at the top of his game. I do digress, but the structure of financial markets, having evolved so momentously starting with the Greenspan era, has never been as fragile.

When the Treasury market is the foundation and Treasuries are crumbling, U.S. and global market structures have serious structural fragility. Benchmark MBS yields surged 56 bps this week to 5.91%, the largest jump since the week of March 13, 2020 (Fed ratcheting up pandemic crisis lending facilities). From Tuesday's low to Friday's high, MBS yields spiked 65 bps.

April 11 - Bloomberg: "The Fannie Mae 30-year current-coupon spread to the 5/10-year blend widened 6 bps to +160 as the U.S. Treasury 10-year yield rose 11 bps to 4.29% and volatility rose. The spread was the highest in more than 13 months. Volatility was the highest in about 18 months."

"Mortgage rates slingshot higher as tariff uncertainty roils markets." "Brutal Week Sees Junk Debt Refinancing Costs Double in 2025." "Cracks Are Forming in CLO Market as ETFs on Record Selling Spree." "Investors lose $25bn in leveraged ETFs in sector's biggest meltdown." "'The World Is Different Now': Market Convulsions Hit Wall Street."

Junk bond yields were up 96 bps in seven sessions to 8.58%, trading this week to the high since October 2023. Leveraged loan prices traded to lows since July 2023. "Private Credit" has serious unfolding issues, with ramifications for "subprime" lending throughout.

April 11 - Bloomberg (Rachel Graf): "As the US leveraged-loan market had no pricings or launches this week amid the ongoing tariff turmoil, the sector was among those from which investors fled to seek safer ground. US leveraged loan funds had a record $6.5 billion pulled from them in the week through April 9."

Muni (AAA) yields were up 89 bps w-t-d at Wednesday's close, before ending the week 66 bps higher at 3.39%.

April 8 - Bloomberg (Aashna Shah and Shruti Date Singh): "A wave of tariff-induced selling pressure hit the municipal bond market on Monday, leading to the worst-performing day in more than three decades. A benchmark index of municipal bonds dropped 2.85% on Monday, the biggest daily decline since at least 1994… The historic rout caused several deals to be postponed and wiped out total gains for this year… 'Monday's session signaled a major risk-off move in the market,' Kimberly Olsan, senior fixed-income portfolio manager for NewSquare Capital LLC, wrote…"

April 9 - Bloomberg (Amanda Albright): "Municipal-bond yields surged another 20 bps Wednesday morning as the state and local debt market sees a continued steep selloff. The rout drove the 10-year AAA benchmark to 3.7%, the highest since at least 2011… Patrick Haskell, head of municipal bonds at BlackRock… said the US state and local debt market hit 'panic levels' Monday and Tuesday. Investors were 'searching for liquidity,' he said."

When America sneezes, the world catches a cold. What's the prognosis when the U.S. has a malignant tumor?

Already fragile, EM bonds suffered a further spike in yields. Dollar-denominated EM bonds were under notable selling pressure - indicative of intense deleveraging. Ten-year yields surged 57 bps in Colombia, 49 bps in Turkey, 48 bps in Philippines and Mexico, 46 bps in Indonesia, 38 bps in Chile, and 24 bps in Brazil. With the yen gaining 2.4% this week (vs. $), there was intense pressure on levered yen "carry trades" - likely including dollar-denominated EM bonds.

Local currency EM bonds suffered the dreaded double-whammy - hefty bond and currency losses. Versus the yen, the Indonesian rupiah fell 3.7%, the Peruvian sol 3.6%, Indian rupee 3.2%, Brazilian real 2.7%, Philippine peso 2.6%, South African rand 2.5%, Chinese renminbi 2.5%, Argentine peso 2.4%, and Turkish lira 2.1%.

Monday's market bloodbath was notable. "Emerging Stocks Sink Most Since 2008 as Tariff Turmoil Deepens." "Stocks in Hong Kong plunged 13.2% for their worst day since 1997" (AP). "MSCI's main emerging equity index slid 7.9%, its biggest drop since the 2008 global financial crisis" (Bloomberg). Major index losses included Taiwan's Taiex 9.7%, Japan's Nikkei 7.8%, South Korea's Kospi 5.6%, China's CSI 300 7.0%, Philippines PSEi 4.3%, Australia's S&P/ASX 200 4.2%, Malaysia's KLCI 4.2%, and India's Nifty 50 4.0%.

Germany's DAX recovered from a 10% plunge to end Monday's session 4.2% lower. Also recovering from steep losses, France's CAC40 closed the day down 2.3%.

The dollar Index sank 2.8%, trading Friday to a two-year low. Levered "carry trades" in U.S. debt instruments financed in low yielding currencies (i.e., yen, Swiss franc, euro) had a rotten week. Algorithmic models would have had essentially zero probability of 10-year Treasury yields spiking 50 bps - as the dollar sank almost 3%.

It's simply hard to believe how messed up things were early in the week. Stocks enjoyed a historic one-day rally and big up week. Can we now just put "Liberation Day" on the south lawn behind us? The tariff placard, the calculations, the propaganda? Can we erase from memory global markets in meltdown mode?

Left for dead, the "Trump put" made a dramatic comeback. When markets again approach the precipice, everyone - or at least the hedge funds - will wait anxiously for THE SIGNAL: "THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY!!! DJT"

April 7 - Financial Times (Costas Mourselas and Antoine Gara): "Shares in billionaire Bill Ackman's main investment vehicle have fallen 15% this year as Donald Trump's trade war hits the portfolio of one of his most ardent Wall Street backers. The drop in Pershing Square Holdings… comes as the financier has turned sour on some of the US president's policies in a major public reversal. The trust's share price fell more than 3% on Monday. 'If… on April ninth we launch economic nuclear war on every country in the world, business investment will grind to a halt, consumers will close their wallets and pocket books, and we will severely damage our reputation with the rest of the world that will take years and potentially decades to rehabilitate,' he said on X on Sunday."

What a difference two days can make…

April 9 - The Hill (Tara Suter): "Billionaire hedge fund investor Bill Ackman on Wednesday praised President Trump's decision to implement a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs against foreign trading partners, with the exception of China. 'This was brilliantly executed by @realDonaldTrump,' Ackman wrote on the social platform X. 'Textbook, Art of the Deal.'"

The hedge fund community had quickly landed in dire straits. Ackman's "Art of the Deal" compliment was like an individual who just landed an incredible deal praising the other side's savvy negotiating tactics. The great negotiator was forced to show his hand - to expose his point of vulnerability. China, witnessing the weakness of its adversary, was quietly triumphal. With steely resolve: "Got him just where we want him."

April 10 - Wall Street Journal (Bertrand Benoit and Kim Mackrael): "Relief spread around the world on Thursday after President Trump suspended enforcement of some of his global tariffs, but the U-turn raised the question of whether big concessions on trade need to be made to mollify an American leader who had been humbled by the market… 'Everyone will likely conclude that [Trump's] credibility as a negotiator has diminished,' said Moritz Schularick, head of the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, a think tank. 'Next time, people will believe him even less and will consider at what point he might buckle again. It certainly hasn't become easier for the U.S. to negotiate.'"

April 8 - CNBC (Jeff Cox): "Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Tuesday the U.S. holds a substantial advantage over China as the two nations exchange threats in a burgeoning trade war. 'I think it was a big mistake, this Chinese escalation, because they're playing with a pair of twos,' Bessent said… 'What do we lose by the Chinese raising tariffs on us? We export one-fifth to them of what they export to us, so that is a losing hand for them.'"

If Xi Jinping is playing with a "pair of twos," what hand might President think he's playing with?

April 8 - Reuters (Laurie Chen, Kevin Yao and David Kirton): "Beijing, feeling boxed into a corner by the United States' intensifying tariff assault on China and any country that buys or assembles Chinese goods, is bracing for an economic war of attrition…. 'Whoever surrenders first becomes the victim,' said a Chinese policy adviser… 'It's a matter of who can hold out longer.'"

"Markets Plummet as Tariff-War Woes Fuel Exodus From US Assets." "US Treasuries sell-off deepens as 'safe haven' status challenged." "Treasuries Selloff Ramps Up as Investors Spurn US Long-End Bonds." "Monday's Jump in 30-Year Treasury Yields Suggests Doubts Over Safety of U.S. Assets." "U.S. Bond Sell-Off Is Another Worrisome Echo of the Liz Truss Fiasco."

I can't imagine an objective of greater priority for Xi Jinping and China's communist party than to see the downfall of so-called U.S. "exorbitant privilege." This competitive advantage has provided incredible benefits to its global superpower rival for decades - compliments of the world's safe haven Treasury market (and U.S. financial markets more generally) coupled with the globe's dependable reserve currency. Now, Donald Trump has unwittingly exposed U.S. Bubble fragilities, vulnerability combined with reckless policymaking, which places U.S. markets, the American economy, and the dollar at great peril.

China has company in wanting to see Trump's America taken down several notches: Russia, North Korea, Iran and, unfortunately, much of the "Global South." Even (former) allies likely today recognize the leverage that would be gained from the U.S. suffering some comeuppance.

April 11 - Bloomberg (Masaki Kondo): "The decline of the US's credibility as a safe haven for financial assets is going to cause issues in the market long-term, according to Westpac… Wednesday's 'price action for fixed income markets, specifically Treasuries, has no historical precedent,' Martin Whetton, head of financial market strategy at Westpac, wrote… 'It is not so much the range - wide and choppy, but the nature of the violent unwind and fall in liquidity, or actually the absence of the liquidity that is concerning.' 'The age of US exceptionalism - at least financially - has come to an end.' Creditors' willingness to finance US deficits has decreased. It's 'startling and a sharp warning' that money didn't scramble to secure dollar funding via the basis markets to buy Treasuries and the dollar for safety."

I've viewed "US exceptionalism" as a key element of peak Bubble delusion. A bulletproof Treasury market and resilient dollar have afforded the U.S. phenomenal advantages. We've operated virtually without constraint - debt growth, fiscal deficits, consumption, current account deficits, asset inflation and Bubbles, deindustrialization, a services-based economy, and unlimited liquidity to pursue whatever technology advancement or financial whim to galvanize highly speculative financial markets.

And when Bubbles inevitably falter, the Fed has enjoyed open-ended capacity to reflate - from Bernanke's Trillion to Powell's $5 TN - a backstop fundamental to U.S. market exceptionalism. Truth be told, "US exceptionalism" was financed by endless liquidity, much of it originating from leveraged speculation backstopped by the Federal Reserve. It inflated into history's greatest Bubble, with things going completely off the rails post-Covid.

I hear and read some seasoned market players confidently asserting that the U.S. has a dominant position vis-à-vis China. The U.S. economy and financial system are "hands down" stronger and more resilient. But this view ignores U.S. Bubble fragilities - financial, economic, and social.

April 8 - Bloomberg: "China pledged to retaliate against Donald Trump's latest tariff threat and mobilized state organs to send a message of resilience, raising the risk of a prolonged trade war between the world's two largest economies. 'The US threat to escalate tariffs on China is a mistake on top of a mistake,' the Chinese Ministry of Commerce said… 'If the US insists on its own way, China will fight to the end.'"

The U.S. and China both have a tremendous amount to lose. We can only pray cooler heads prevail. But Beijing today has a lot to gain. A unique opportunity has landed in Xi Jinping's lap. A global financial crisis would present challenges, but it would clearly be blamed directly on Donald Trump. The Chinese population would rally around Xi and the communist party, blame deflected from their own formidable policy blunders and mismanagement.

A Trump global crisis would also afford China the opportunity to expand its circle and global influence. One rival superpower stacking up friends and allies - with the other floundering and humbled. Beijing might also calculate that the U.S. in disarray would be less compelled to exert influence in Asia - and less likely to come to Taiwan's defense. A world with an impaired America would be a playground for China and Russia. A world without U.S. "exorbitant privilege" would be a dream come true. Just imagine the elation when Xi and Putin huddle together.

If U.S./China trade war analysis lacks sufficient challenge, give current market analysis a whirl. April option expiration is next Friday, so I'll take a cowardly approach and state conclusively that "anything could happen." Bonds, fixed income, and currencies will remain the big story. The world is in the initial phase of a wrenching deleveraging that I expect to last months and likely years.

We can think in terms of the first round having quickly escalated to the brink of markets "seizing up," only to be pulled away from the precipice by 90-Day Tariff Pause. Somewhat of a recovery is possible, fueled by short squeezes and the unwind of hedges. But markets have been severely impaired. No putting the toothpaste back in the tube. Confidence has been badly shaken. The extreme degree of uncertainty (i.e., Trump, US/China trade war, economic, inflation risks...) ensures an environment inhospitable to leverage.

The leveraged speculating community has been fractured. The game works well when everyone has conviction to stay in the game. The game turns problematic when interests diverge - each billionaire for himself - dog eat dog. With deleveraging having gained powerful momentum, liquidity will be an ongoing issue. Like the August yen "carry trade" unwind, there's been a mad rush to put on hedges and bring down leverage. But actual deleveraging of portfolios and strategies will take time - and ensures clouds hang over markets.

Furthermore, in the markets the past couple weeks there has been a distinct look of algorithmic models being blown to smithereens. An eruption of global deleveraging - and yet Treasury yields spike 50 bps and the dollar sinks 2.8%. The virtually impossible is suddenly commonplace, a nightmare dynamic for the crowded population of over-confident quant traders. Moreover, the newfound high risk Treasury dynamic must be causing tons of grief in lots of levered strategies that hold Treasuries to hedge portfolios of risk assets.

Spiking Treasury yields is a major market, financial and economic issue - at home and abroad. Does Beijing do a cold, strategic calculation - and go for the jugular? "Fight to the end" doesn't sound like a bluff.

For Posterity:

April 7 - Wall Street Journal (Gerard Baker): "When I worked in Tokyo in the 1990s, a Japanese colleague told me a story about her father's defining experience with Americans. In the days after Japan surrendered at the end of World War II, he was a young boy living in a small town. As American troops moved to occupy the country, the mood was one of panicked terror. The Japanese had been told by their leaders that the victorious Americans would murder, rape and pillage just as their own troops had treated their defeated enemies-though of course the Japanese people didn't know that. Eventually American trucks rolled through the town and he and his family cowered indoors, awaiting the inevitable savagery. They watched as GIs jumped down from their vehicles and began placing heavy boxes on the street. At first they assumed the boxes contained some terrible weapon… but when the troops were gone and the boxes remained, some of the more curious and braver kids ventured out and began opening them. Inside were layers and layers of chocolate bars, candy, and other treats the near-starving Japanese had not seen in years. The memory lived with him his whole life, and the story moved me greatly, as I have traveled across the world and become a proud American myself."

April 7 - Bloomberg: "China's central bank said it will provide support to a sovereign fund when needed as it firmly supports its decision to buy more stocks. The People's Bank of China will step up funding aid via a re-lending program to Central Huijin Investment Ltd. when it's necessary, in order to safeguard the stability of capital markets, it said…"

April 9 - Reuters (Qiaoyi Li and Ryan Woo): "China's consumer prices fell for the second straight month in March while factory-gate deflation worsened, as an escalating U.S. trade war heightened worries about mounting piles of unsold exports that could drive domestic prices even lower… The consumer price index (CPI) dropped 0.1% last month from a year earlier…"

Europe Watch:

April 9 - Financial Times (Olaf Storbeck): "The European Central Bank faces an economic environment in which 'some of the worst-case scenarios that we had identified are materialising', according to José Luis Escrivá, governor of the Bank of Spain and a member of the ECB's governing council… Escrivá warned that tariffs imposed by Donald Trump were triggering a 'very significant negative shock on economic activity'. He suggested US policies could bring into question the dollar's status as a reserve currency and haven. Escrivá said the precise fallout from the sweeping measures… was 'still uncertain', adding that monetary policymakers were 'monitoring it closely'."

April 8 - Politico (Balazs Koranyi and Francesco Canepa): "U.S. tariffs will be a massive drag on global growth and the European Central Bank will do its part to support Europe but the solution lies in more unity on the continent, ECB policymaker Joachim Nagel said… 'Global growth prospects have deteriorated massively,' Nagel said… 'Monetary policy in Europe will do its part… We are already well on our way to achieving our inflation target this year.'"

April 6 - Financial Times (Anne-Sylvaine Chassany and Olaf Storbeck): "Germany's chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz has warned of the 'dramatic' impact of US tariffs on global markets and on Germany, in his first comments since US President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping levies against the EU. 'The situation on the international equity and bond markets is dramatic and threatens to deteriorate further,' he said... 'It is therefore more urgent than ever for Germany to restore its international competitiveness as quickly as possible.'"

Social, Political, Environmental, Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

April 7 - Financial Times (Kenza Bryan and Jana Tauschinski): "Arctic sea ice hit a record low for the end of the region's winter last month, in a stark sign of how climate change is opening up the North Pole to a geopolitical race for military and energy exploration… Military and commercial vessels, particularly from Russia, are stepping up activity in the region, some of which is expected to be ice-free during summer within a decade, said Rebecca Pincus, former director of the Wilson Center's Polar Institute. US President Donald Trump's desire to take control of Greenland has increased attention on the area's critical minerals and importance for trade and military strategy."
