data.title: Kraken Robotics: Expect A Strong Quarter
data.description:
Over the past three years, in a baleful contest for Black Sea supremacy, Ukraine's un-crewed underwater vessels (UUVs) have consistently bettered Russia's once heralded surface fleet. These UUVs are cheap, quick to build, and --as they avoid the risk of sailor casualties-- far more “politically convenient” than the typical submarine.

The world took notice, and as Western militaries eagerly shift large portions of their naval budgets to un-crewed underwater vessels (UUVs), a tiny micro-cap named Kraken Robotics (TSXV:PNG:CA) (KRKNF) is entering the proverbial sweet spot.

Kraken is a Newfoundland, Canada-based company that develops and manufactures devices for underwater exploration, with segments in marine robotics, sensors, and --perhaps most pertinently for this report-- subsea batteries for UUVs.

The firm's unique silicon encapsulation process makes their SeaPower batteries less heavy and less likely to corrode underwater than traditional pressure-housed batteries. More intriguingly, this method also allows them to tolerate pressures up to 660 Bar, the equivalent of 6000 meters ocean depth, thus providing far deeper sea activity and up to twice the energy density at 46% less the weight.

The world should be running to its door and, indeed, the Smart Money is. On April 10, Kraken announced new orders totaling CAD11 million for its SeaPower-brand batteries, bringing its new battery orders since January 1st to CAD45 million. Quite impressive for a company with a $417 million market cap that only took in CAD19.2 million (USD13.8 million) in total revenue last quarter.

Kraken's Market

The company's demand is mainly coming from two clients –Anduril Industries and Huntington Ingalls (HII) (with the former providing the vast lion's share). Anduril’s Dive-LD, a sleek 3-ton UUV set within a 3D-printed exterior, can operate at 6,000 meters of ocean depth because of Kraken. The Dive got Replicator Program funding via the Navy’s PMS 394 and was later tapped for the second tranche. Each Dive-LD is presently equipped with Kraken’s SeaPower batteries, sensors and utility software.

In addition to its existing Quincy, MA facility, Anduril is setting up a new 150,000 sq. ft factory in Quonset Point, RI (expected to open in summer 2025) that will allow the firm to ultimately roll out 200 Dive-LDs per year(!).

Separately, Anduril received a separate US Navy contract for $18.6 million, following its “swim-off” win for a “Large Displacement Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (LDUUV). The desire to build XL UUVs –ones that could arguably displace some portion of a nation's submarine budget—has grown dramatically over the past 18 months. Ukraine’s successes against its enemy’s Crimean fleet and the crisis over the ballooning costs of US Virginia-class subs last fall [1] are exhibit A & B as to why western navies must seek to find faster, cheaper solutions that can cope with the expendability levels that will come with peer-to-peer contests.

Last Spring retired General Mark Milley reportedly said that 33% of the US armed forces will be robotic in 8 years, and in the fall Chief of Naval Operations Adm. Lisa Franchetti openly discussed Project 33, an agenda focused on scaling up and integrating “robotic and autonomous systems across the US fleet by 2027.”

Besides the Dive, there is the Ghost Shark. Two years ago, Anduril unveiled its Ghost Shark XL-UUV prototype as part of Australia’s “Advanced Strategic Capabilities Accelerator” consortium, and the success led to a $90 million co-development agreement, whereby Anduril will deliver three Ghost Sharks by June 2025. As an XL, each Ghost Shark relies on a far larger SeaPower battery than the Dive-LD. Australia plans a broader “fleet” of Sharks for surveillance and strike, with a plant in Sydney dedicated to the buildout.

In addition, there is the Lionfish, the US Navy’s name for its Small Unmanned Undersea Vehicle program, based on Huntington Ingalls’ (HII) Remus 300 design, with the purpose of intelligence gathering beside its expeditionary mine countermeasures company. The drone is small, weighing roughly 150 pounds, and can be carried by two sailors.

Though an interesting use case, the Lionfish program could amount to Kraken revenues of no more than $40 million by 2029, so the singular client remains Anduril. The dominance of one big client is typically a worry for any micro-cap, particularly in a situation where the product risks becoming a commodified. I don’t see this as a problem here. The SeaPower battery offers unique performance metrics in terms of sea depth and energy per weight, and it enjoys a durable patent. In the flurry of scaled up delivery that Project 33 and Anduril’s RI factory specs suggest, I believe an essential place in the Anduril supply chain is an incredible tailwind.

Financials: The Tell is the Revenue upgrade

With a P/S of 5 and P/E [GAAP] of 53, KRKNF remains expensive --even after the late March/ early April drawdown, though its financial position certainly looks strong. Short-term assets stand at CAD67.66 million with liabilities at CAD24.38 million, while long-term assets of CAD33.53 million versus CAD17.88 million also is favorable.

Kraken’s next quarterly earnings call is reportedly April 30, 2025. It is likely to be far better than the last one.

Back in November, Kraken offered up a weak quarter, with Q3 revenue of CAD19.55M, down 3.9% year-over-year, its adjusted EBITDA down 6%, and net income down 29% (though that was due to tougher financing costs from a new credit facility). The very “Meh” quarter certainly muddied things, though the firm's YTD’s net income growth of 117% may keep investors in their seats.

This bit of management guidance may have also helped:

“Our annual revenue and adjusted EBITDA financial guidance remains unchanged. Kraken expects revenue between CAD90.0 million and CAD100.0 million and Adjusted EBITDA in the CAD18.0 million and CAD24.0 million range. Capital and intangible asset expenditures in 2024 are now expected to be in the CAD4.0 million and CAD5.0 million range (versus CAD6.0 million and CAD7.0 million range previously)."

With a topline of CAD63.2 million for the first three quarters, that FY declarative can only mean impressive 4Q revenues of CAD27 to $37 million.

Indeed, year-to-date 2024 revenues nearly match 2023’s full 4 quarters, and the present TTM is CAD91.20 million. Gross margins have been climbing to an average of 49.37% these past three quarters. Of that estimated CAD90 to 100 million, about 75–80 million should be batteries and sensors, well up from $56 million in 2023.

Management’s guidance range for the next quarter, also sees a nice drop-off in capital expenditures:

During earnings, management did highlight new product revenue --the sale of a Katfish to an East Asian navy and another to a NATO country; the large uptake of their SeaPower® subsea batteries; sales of its RMDS system to Canada.

It also spoke of future demand in its mine warfare segment after they held demos for nearly a dozen navies at the Naval MCM conference in Halifax: “Historically, these demos have driven future sales and with our growing track record of success and relationships in this area, we believe we are well positioned to win our fair share of these future large programs.”

Anduril: Deep Pockets

Whatever may come of this new interest in mine warfare, the central driver for revenue will remain UUV battery demand from Anduril Industries.

Two more Ghost Sharks are to be delivered to Australia’s Navy by June 2025, and, with the first produced by June 2024, well ahead of schedule, these two will also likely come in before the deadline. The Ghost Shark program is at the center of Australia's USD4.62 billion investment in subsea warfare capabilities and autonomous maritime power projection, with another six to eight slated in the coming years.

In terms of the US Navy’s Dive-DL: Anduril's existing Quincy facility can presently build 12 hulls per year and possibly expand that to 24 with extra shifts if needed. The RI facility is expected to open in 2025, with its modular, hyperscale capacity expected to be 200 hulls a year.

The plant is clearly connected to the Power 33 program, embracing as it does the autonomous systems mandate, as well as the understanding that surface armadas in the near future will probably operate with an outer edge of “attributable” UUVs probing at their vanguard.

Based on the facility capacity and the stated effort to "catch up" to China by 2027, one can speculate on the "hulls per year" construction for both the Ghost Shark and Dive-DL.

At present, with FY 2024 revenues coming in at the guide's median CAD95 million, we are modeling CAD145 million in revenues for FY 2025 and CAD203 million for FY 2026 respectively.

This is primarily due to the massive Dive-LD, Ghost Shark scale up with assorted Lionfish/Katfish/mine warfare buy-ins.

With the Ghost Shark build-out going to 10 by 2028 and the smaller Dive-LD's delivery going to, say, 180 by 2028, we are in the midst of an exponential leap in Kraken's revenue, one that makes it a new type of company.

Kraken’s COO Nat Spencer, an industry veteran who heads up the battery division, was revealing in a recent interview on the shockingly large UUV demand that has reshaped his once sleepy segment over the past few years.

Of course, Kraken’s core competency in pressure-neutral batteries makes it an obvious take-over target for Anduril, which would likely save $150+ million in battery input costs over the next several years alone. (Acquisitions have been at the core of "VC arriviste/ patriot" Palmer Luckey's Anduril's strategy, with the 2017 takeover of Dive Technologies, the true incubator for the Dive-LD, being the most obvious).

Luckey has deep connections to Silicon Valley’s VC class. In February, Anduril raised $2.5 billion at a valuation of $28 billion, money clearly not needed for their Ohio or RI plants which are done or nearing completion. Indeed, Anduril saw its own internal revenues double in 2024 to about $1 billion, with further contract value approaching $1.5 billion, so this extra tapped money is likely for adding facilities and roll-ups.

Risks:

The challenges to Kraken? The firm certainly faces many risks. The sonar ecosystem is a challenge; the Katfish is seeing purchases, but the space is filled with able competitors; and while their batteries offer compelling performance specs, they can be out-priced by a different mix that, say, operates with a combo of lithium batteries and diesel generators.

Most daunting is its singular relationship with Anduril, as any shift away to a new battery supplier would be catastrophic to Kraken’s high valuation.

The Trump trade uncertainty also poses some danger to profitability. As a Canadian company with all of its sought-after batteries made solely in Germany, it would experience the full force of US tariffs later this year. This would ding results going forward.

The bull case? There are no changing horses at this moment. Australia's “Advanced Strategic Capabilities Accelerator” and America's Replicator Program and Project 33 are mission-critical endeavors that will brook no diversions. Anduril and its “fast, modular” platforms (in Sydney and Rhode Island) will be what the US and Australian navies deploy to level up their maritime autonomous systems in the Indo-Pacific.

Kraken, as a critical supplier and fellow traveler, is “in for the penny and in for the pound.”

[1] US Navy's new submarines are 'in crisis' as costs balloon by $17 billion, lawmaker says.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Union Pacific: Not Ready To Buy, With Tariffs In Play And Valuation A Concern
data.description:
In terms of market capitalization, the largest railroad operator in North America is Union Pacific Corporation (UNP). As of this writing, it boasts a market value of $135.34 billion. With such a large size from a market cap perspective also comes a large size in terms of revenue. However, in recent years, financial performance figures have been mixed. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all been stuck in a fairly narrow range.

Unfortunately, broader economic uncertainty is likely responsible for this. But that doesn't stop management from continuing to invest in current operations and growth prospects. Furthermore, management has succeeded in buying back a significant number of shares in recent years, plus it has regularly paid out billions of dollars annually to shareholders in the form of dividends. Compared to other similar firms, the company looks more or less fairly valued, or perhaps just a bit pricey. At the end of the day, this relative valuation, combined with how shares are priced on an absolute basis, makes this a ‘hold’ in my book.

Taking a ride with Union Pacific

The management team at Union Pacific describes the company as a major player in the North American railroad industry. The company currently has 52,372 miles of rail to its name. This includes 32,880 route miles, 7,116 main line miles that fall outside of route miles, 3,526 passing lines and turnout miles, and 8,550 switching and classification yard line miles. Gradually over the years, management has increased the size of the company's footprint. The number of miles that the business had at the end of 2024 was up from the 51,954 that the business had just two years earlier. To be clear, this is not a rapidly growing company. But that's because growth is incredibly expensive, especially when talking about any populated areas that the firm might want to expand into.

The firm transports a wide array of goods in the states in which it operates. This includes over 9,000 car loads each year of ethanol, one train per day of coal, over 9,000 car loads each year of grain products, and over 6,000 carloads of intermodal units that carry all sorts of industrial and consumer goods. As I mentioned already, growth for the company has not really been a thing in recent years. Take revenue as an example. In 2022, revenue was $24.88 billion. This ended up dropping to $24.12 billion in 2023 before improving slightly to $24.25 billion in 2024.

To best understand the company and its performance, we would be wise to look at where its revenue is coming from and how that has changed over the past few years. As an example, the firm has seen a nice increase in sales associated with grain and grain products. From 2022 through 2024, these commodities went from generating $3.60 billion of sales for the company to generating $3.83 billion worth. This came as the number of carloads associated with them grew from 798,000 to 850,000. Fertilizer was also a growth area for the firm, with revenue climbing from $712 million to $811 million. This was thanks to a rise in fertilizer carloads from 190,000 to 213,000. There has been a gradual increase in average revenue per car associated with fertilizer products, but grain and grain products revenue per car has remained shockingly flat.

One problem area for the company has been with coal and renewables. A drop in the number of car loads from 885,000 to 702,000 and a decline from $2,410 per car to $2,113 per car over the last three years has pushed revenue associated with these products down from $2.13 billion to $1.48 billion. Inclement weather and power outages caused some of this pain. However, we have also seen a general decline in the demand of coal in recent years. We don't have production data for 2024 as of yet. But we do know that US coal production declined by 2.7% in 2023 compared to the previous year. This came even as the number of producing coal mines increased from 548 to 560. The decline in revenue per car was likely in relation to a decline in pricing for the commodity also.

Coal and renewables weren't the only weak spots for the firm. Over the last three years, metals and minerals revenue dropped, as did revenue associated with forest products. These declines are almost certainly because of reduced construction activity in the residential space. After all, high-interest rates aimed at combating inflation, combined with the inflationary impact of making homes less affordable, should have caused a reduction in the demand for products like this. Interestingly, automotive revenue jumped from $2.26 billion to $2.45 billion. Management reported that during this time the average revenue per car associated with automotives grew from $2,902 to $2,976, while the number of revenue carloads popped from 778,000 to 824,000. According to Cox Automotive, the number of new vehicles sold in the US grew from 13.8 million to 16 million over this window of time. Around the time that the pandemic began, and the world shut down, there was a big decrease in semiconductors and prices of new vehicles skyrocketed. This is just a return to more normal levels compared to what occurred prior to the pandemic. However, with some estimates calling for a three million unit drop this year because of the trade wars that the US began, I wouldn't be surprised if 2025 ends up being a down year for the company in this regard.

Even though automotive sales suffered, the firm did see revenue involving energy and specialized markets grow from $2.39 billion to $2.69 billion. This was thanks to a rise in the number of carloads from 552,000 to 607,000 and was also attributable to an increase in the average revenue per car from $4,320 to $4,426. Unfortunately, the intermodal space saw some weakness. Even as the number of carloads jumped from 3.32 million to 3.36 million, revenue dropped from $5.16 billion to $4.71 billion. This was thanks to a decline in the average revenue per car from $1,656 to $1,404.

Unfortunately, these mixed results have caused results on the bottom line to be mixed also. From 2022 to 2023, net profits for the company fell from $7 billion to $6.38 billion. But then, in 2024, they had risen to $6.75 billion. Operating cash flow has remained range bound between $8.38 billion and $9.36 billion. Last year, it totaled $9.35 billion. We saw a similar range when it came to adjusted operating cash flow, which adjusts for changes in working capital. Similarly, EBITDA has remained in a fairly narrow range of between $11.46 billion and $12.22 billion. Last year, it was $12.16 billion.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management has not really provided any guidance when it comes to revenue. They did say that earnings per share would likely expand at either a high single digit rate or a low double-digit rate. It's unclear the impact that tariffs will have on the firm. In all likelihood, they will worsen financial results and necessitate a downward revision in financial performance. However, the company continues to make major investments in growth prospects. From 2021 through 2024, for instance, the firm engaged in approximately 200 track construction projects that, combined, generate over $800 million of revenue annually. It also has an active pipeline of over 200 construction projects that could bring on another $1.5 billion in annual revenue.

Management seems to be particularly interested in certain key markets. The grain and grain products space, for instance, is ripe with opportunity. This is because around 1 billion bushels of product is produced each year. Management is also interested in continued production expansions involving the petrochemicals industry. Truck conversions are also on its radar, as is nearshoring and changes associated with the USMCA in recent years that have spurred $140 billion in investments. Unfortunately, all this tariff situation at least temporarily throws some of this, especially when it comes to the nearshoring and USMCA investments because management specifically identified this is an opportunity involving Mexico.

Unless something has changed from a planning perspective, the company does currently intend to allocate around $3.4 billion toward capital projects this year. $1.9 billion of this will involve infrastructure replacement such as new rail, ties, and ballast. $0.6 billion will involve capacity and commercial facilities centered around things like intermodal activities and manifest terminals. The firm is investing $0.5 billion into its locomotives and other equipment like freight cars. On the locomotive side, this one involved modernization initiatives. And finally, it is planning to allocate $0.4 billion toward technology and other miscellaneous investments aimed at improving operations and customer experience.

This does not mean that the company is ignoring shareholders. In fact, in addition to growing, the business is also focused on returning capital to its investors. From 2020 through 2024, it has allocated $34.5 billion towards shareholder returns. This has involved $15 billion in dividends and $19.5 billion in share buybacks.

Personally, I would prefer that the company focus on other growth initiatives, especially over dividends. But the fact that the firm is able to allocate so much toward these activities while still maintaining a net leverage ratio that, as the chart above illustrates, places it around the low end of the competition, is encouraging.

Speaking of competition, we should discuss valuation. In the chart above, you can see how Union Pacific is valued on an absolute basis using results from both 2023 and 2024. In the table below, you can see how the firm is priced compared to four similar enterprises. Both a price to operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, you can see that three of the four companies ended up being cheaper than our prospect is. But on a price to earnings basis, two of the four companies are cheaper. This means that Union Pacific is between fairly valued and slightly pricey compared to the similar firms. But as I mentioned already, its net leverage ratio is near the lower end of the spectrum, which I believe justifies some wiggle room.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Union Pacific Corporation 20.1 14.8 13.6
Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) 24.2 17.1 15.0
Canadian National Railway Co. (CNI) 18.9 12.5 12.1
CSX Corp. (CSX) 15.2 10.1 10.0
Norfolk Southern Corporation (NSC) 18.2 11.8 11.9

We don't really know the full impact that tariffs will have on the company. We can say that it will reduce demand for the firm's services, especially if the US goes into a recession. That does seem increasingly likely. Management doesn't provide a breakdown of data regarding Canada. But it does provide data regarding Mexico. From 2022 to 2024, revenue involving Mexico has grown from $2.7 billion to $3 billion. This means that it has expanded from 10.9% of sales to 12.4%. This is a sizable chunk of revenue that could be particularly impacted depending on how the tariff situation evolves.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, Union Pacific is not a great business. But it's not an awful business by any means. Even though financial performance has been mixed in recent years, there have been key aspects of the business that have grown nicely. Management continues to invest in growth initiatives while returning significant amounts of capital directly to shareholders. Given how the stock is priced and the uncertainty that we have when it comes to tariffs, I would argue that it does currently make for a better ‘hold’ than it does a 'buy.'

data.title: The Hershey Company: Sweet Change In Bitter Times
data.description:
The Hershey Company (HSY) is known for its strong brands, such as Hershey itself, but also Reese's and recent acquisitions such as Dot's Pretzels, SkinnyPop and recently LesserEvil.

The robust brand portfolio, high profit margins and growing dividend make this stock potentially interesting for dividend investors.

However, the price has been in free fall since 2023 and has already fallen by 40%. This is due to the sharply increased cocoa prices. The cocoa price tripled to a record high. This makes it difficult for Hershey to achieve strong profit margins without sacrificing volume. The recent price drop may offer an interesting opportunity to get in cheaply, provided that cocoa prices stabilize again.

This reminds me of 2020, when demand for fuels and oil plummeted. Oil stocks plummeted, and it was a good time to get in. Many investors feared that work-from-home would continue and that oil prices would not recover. Now, five years later, the opposite has been proven. Share prices of Occidental Petroleum, Chevron, and Exxon Mobil have all risen sharply.

I see a similar scenario for Hershey.

Cacao Prices Could Stabilize

The reason behind Hershey’s share price drop is that cocoa prices have risen sharply. This has put a lot of pressure on margins. In 2024, the cocoa price rose to $12,000 per ton (three times as much as in 2023). This is the result of a combination of both structural and temporary factors.

Hershey had to deal with supply problems in the Ivory Coast and Ghana. Together, these countries account for more than 60% of the world’s production. The extreme drought caused by El Niño led to diseases such as the fungal disease “black pod”, which affected entire harvests.

These countries also struggle with poor agricultural infrastructure and aging cocoa trees. This is because these farmers receive low prices for their cocoa and therefore have few resources to invest.

This caused a sharp drop in production, which meant that demand could not be met. However, demand remained strong, which caused prices to rise sharply. For Hershey (85% of its revenue depends on chocolate) this is a major problem. Cocoa accounts for half of the raw material costs for a chocolate bar. Hershey has hedged the risk of rising purchasing costs well by concluding forward contracts, but these expire. This year, new purchases are being made at significantly higher prices, which will put considerable pressure on margins.

Hershey expects gross profit margins to decline by around 600 to 700 basis points. Gross margins are currently still strong at 54%. But even with new price increases, the margin cannot be restored. Not only Hershey, but also competitors feel this in their margins. Lindt has announced that it will implement significant price increases again. And Mondelez indicates that margins will shrink.

For investors, there seems to be no relief. But we must not forget that cocoa has always been a highly cyclical commodity. High prices attract investment and stimulate replanting, irrigation and sustainability of cocoa plantations. The regulator (Cocobod) has encouraged farmers to replace and replant old trees. The yield of young cocoa trees is much higher than that of the current old cocoa trees. However, farmers face a problem: a farmer’s land rights are linked to the crops he grows. Cutting down the cocoa can therefore mean losing your lease.

There are initiatives to financially support cocoa farmers during the period when their new cocoa trees are maturing. In the Nyame Nnae project, ECOM paid for the rehabilitation and planted plantains and corn between the cocoa trees. Farmers can then sell these crops to recoup their investment and sustain themselves until the cocoa is ripe. It takes about 3 to 4 years for the cocoa trees to mature. This would mean that cocoa prices will decrease in the coming years, because a young tree yields about 2 to 3 times more cocoa than an old cocoa tree. Hershey’s margins will improve as a result, especially if the sales price increases are maintained.

To reduce cocoa price risk, Hershey has diversified its product portfolio with brands such as Dot's Pretzels and SkinnyPop popcorn. These are less dependent on cocoa. And the acquisition of LesserEvil (producer of organic and healthy snacks) fits into the strategy. Chocolate remains the core of the company for the time being, and cocoa prices will therefore continue to have a strong influence on the operating result in the coming years. If weather conditions in Ghana and Ivory Coast improve and harvests increase, the current share price decline is a good time to enter.

Strong Quarterly Figures, But Poor Outlook

Hershey’s results are looking strong at the moment. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Hershey’s revenue increased by 8.7% to $2.9 billion. This was not only due to the price increases, but also to the 6% volume increase. As a result, adjusted earnings per share also increased by 33% to $2.69, but this was mainly due to hedge profits on cocoa purchases. Without this windfall, profits would have been much lower. For the whole of 2024, revenue remained virtually flat at $11.2 billion, while adjusted earnings per share fell by 2.3% to $9.37. The outlook for 2025 is muted, with management expecting revenue growth of just 2% and a sharp decline in earnings per share of around 35%. Gross margin will decline by around 600-700 bps.

The profit decline can also be attributed to higher interest expenses. Earlier this year, Hershey issued $2 billion in new bonds at an average interest rate of 4.8%, paying off old loans of 0.9% and 3.2%. Because of this, interest expenses will rise to $190-$195 million, up from $165 million in 2024. However, its balance sheet is still very strong. Total debt is only $5 billion, and with free cash flow of around $2 billion, its debt is well-manageable. In addition, Hershey is working on a $125 million savings program to increase its margins.

Dividends and Valuation

Hershey has been raising its dividend for 15 years, and in 2024 the dividend rose strongly by 15% to $5.48 per share. At the current share price, that represents a dividend yield of 3.3%. The payout ratio is approaching 50%, a healthy balance between the dividend payout and retaining cash for further growth.

The fall in the share price has made the valuation of the stock more attractive. The forward P/E ratio is 27x, certainly not cheap, but if cocoa prices stabilize, earnings will return to their previous levels and the valuation is attractive now.

European peer Nestlé is more attractively valued, but its debt ratios are less favorable than those of Hershey. And Lindt, on the other hand, is valued much more expensively.

Conclusion

Hershey has had a hard time in recent years due to the sharply increased cocoa prices. And because a large part of its revenue depends on cocoa, this has a major impact on the profit. The profit is expected to fall sharply, and the share has already been significantly corrected for the expected profit decline. The profit margin is still strong and the increased cocoa prices can be partly passed on to the sales price. As soon as cocoa prices decrease, Hershey becomes attractive in the market. The prices are expected to fall in the coming years due to the growth of young cocoa trees and investments in agricultural infrastructure. But if the drought continues for a long time, the risk of the fungal disease "black pod" remains high. Structurally there is a visible improvement, with the risk of extreme weather conditions. Now that the cocoa price is already falling, I see a good buying opportunity for Hershey.

data.title: IXN: Big Tech Is Still Worth Holding Long Term
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares Global Tech ETF (IXN) warrants a hold rating due to the growth potential of its top mega-cap tech holdings despite recent tariff actions. These tech companies either have diversified business segments or are actively taking actions to mitigate tariffs, posturing the IT companies for solid long-term returns. However, IXN has notable downsides including higher fees compared to competitor tech funds. Therefore, while IXN’s top holdings have a strong long-term outlook, investors may find better returns with the peer funds discussed in this article.

Fund Overview and Compared ETFs

IXN is a passive ETF that seeks to capture technology companies globally. With its inception in 2001, the fund has 123 holdings and over $4B in AUM. IXN's holdings include electronics, computer hardware, and software industries within the IT sector. While being a global fund, most of IXN's weight is in U.S. companies (80.85%), followed by companies in Taiwan (5.54%) and Japan (3.34% weight).

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ), Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT), and The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK). These funds are very similar in top holdings, but unlike IXN, exclusively capture U.S. companies. QQQ tracks the largest 100 non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq. VGT captures the IT sector exclusively and therefore, unlike QQQ, lacks any holdings in the consumer discretionary and health care sectors. Finally, XLK tracks IT companies within the S&P 500 Index.

Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

Despite the recent sell-off in big tech, top tech ETFs have seen strong performance over the past decade that surpassed the market overall, as measured by the S&P 500. IXN has a 10-year average annual return of 17.7%, lagging QQQ, VGT, and XLK’s 10-year average annual returns of 18.28%, 18.93%, and 18.84%, respectively.

IXN is the most expensive fund and the smallest by AUM. Because IXN’s top holdings are tech companies with a focus towards growth, the overall dividend yield is minimal. However, IXN has the lowest dividend yield that has also been declining in recent years.

IXN’s top three holdings make up almost 50% of the fund’s total weight. Based on this concentration, the outlook for these mega-cap tech companies will have a massive impact on the fund’s overall performance. Tariff policy represents a significant variable which will have a varying impact on these mega-cap tech companies. These potential impacts with each company’s mitigating actions are discussed further below.

Apple: Moving Manufacturing to Mitigate Risk

The first key consideration is IXN’s top holding, Apple, at almost 18% weight. In the ongoing trade war, U.S. import tariff rates have reached 145% for China. While smartphones were recently exempted from reciprocal tariffs, frequent policy changes risk lifting this exemption. If the exemption is reversed, tariffs will dramatically cut Apple’s potential profits. For example, assuming a declared value of $500 on an iPhone, a 145% tariff would result in a cost of $725. However, Apple’s relationship with China was problematic even before tariff actions. China has accounted for almost 20% of Apple’s total sales for the past several years. This market share has been disrupted by Huawei's own increasing market share, resulting in a -11% YoY growth for Apple’s revenue in China. This declining revenue from China was a major factor in Apple’s overall YoY revenue growth of just 2.6%.

On the bright side, Apple maintains strong profitability with a 24% net income margin. Therefore, while the tech company could potentially absorb some tariff costs, much of the price increase would likely be passed on to the consumer. To mitigate these tariff actions, Apple is taking multiple steps. The first is to increase manufacturing in India which is currently seeing a lower tariff rate. The second risk mitigation is a $500B investment into facilities, increasing manufacturing capacity in the United States. However, the earliest facility in Houston would not open until 2026. Therefore, while Apple is taking actions to mitigate tariff actions, the effects of these actions will take time.

Microsoft: Diversification and Reduced Tariff Sensitivity

The second key factor is the fund’s #2 holding, Microsoft, which carries over 17% of IXN's weight. Compared to Apple, Microsoft is arguably more resilient to tariffs given that its intelligent cloud and software business segments make up a large percentage of the company’s revenue. This resiliency is because these segments are not as heavily tied to manufacturing in other countries like Apple’s products.

However, Microsoft is certainly not invulnerable to tariff actions. Hardware like laptops, tablets, and Xbox entertainment consoles are still subject to tariffs if produced overseas. As a partial result of this reduced tariff sensitivity, Microsoft has only experienced a YTD share price decline of 7%, better than Apple, Nvidia, Amazon (AMZN), and Broadcom (AVGO).

Nvidia: Profitable Business Segments to Weather the Storm

The final factor is Nvidia at 9% weight. Over the last few quarters, a significant portion of Nvidia’s revenue has originated from its data center segment. This business segment includes manufactured powerful chips, many for AI use, that come from Taiwan and South Korea. However, semiconductor chips from Taiwan are exempt from reciprocal tariffs. Additionally, the recent tariff pause impacted all countries except for China. Therefore, like Microsoft, Nvidia is currently postured for strong long-term performance despite recent tariff volatility.

Nvidia has seen a share price drop of 20% YTD driving a forward P/E of just 24. This valuation means that NVDA has a P/E roughly the same as its sector median and 60% below its own 5-year average. Additionally, strong fundamentals posture the company for continued strong growth including a 55% net income margin. Therefore, unless the United States itself sees a significant recession, Nvidia’s profits will likely continue to be strong in the long term.

Current Valuation

Since the beginning of 2025, IXN has seen a -13% share price decline. Due to the recent announcement for a 90-day pause in global tariffs, all funds saw a strong rebound. However, IXN still holds a negative one-year return and is 17% below its all-time high.

Due to this YTD performance, particularly in the tech sector, IXN’s price-to-earnings ratio has recently become more attractive. Additionally, given the long-term strengths of the tech companies already discussed, the IT sector will likely see strong forward growth. However, IXN’s fees remain a negative factor, dragging the fund to lag peer competitors.

Risks to Investors

Due to its concentration on the IT sector, IXN has historically been more volatile than the market overall with a 3-year beta of 1.25 and standard deviation of 23.17%. Two major risk factors are present looking forward. The first is the uncertainty regarding expanded tariff actions. Although the latest move is a 90-day pause in tariffs for nations except for China, policy changes have been frequent, resulting in uncertainty and volatility.

The second rising risk factor is the risk of recession. While the diversified business segments of Microsoft and Nvidia will mitigate these tariffs, rising prices may lead to higher inflation. This possibility has increased the forecast risk of U.S. recession to 45% within the next year, up from 35%. This puts the Federal Reserve in a dilemma where the U.S. may see slowing economic growth and increasing inflation. Such as stagflation condition poses a significant risk to the economy and therefore the tech sector.

Concluding Summary

Despite tariff policies, big tech is poised for long-term growth. Apple, with significant manufacturing activity in China, is actively taking measures to reduce tariff risks. Additionally, Microsoft and Nvidia have diversified business segments that will likely limit negative impacts. However, the rising risk of recession as a result of tariffs remains concerning for the overall market. Therefore, while there may be short-term declines and continued volatility, investing in tech for the long term will likely produce strong returns. While the differences are small, IXN has multiple disadvantages compared to peer tech funds, including a higher expense ratio and a slightly lower dividend yield.
